        Smaller datasets have the potential to cause issues with the data
        adaptive methods, especially the FD method. All bin numbers have been
        precalculated.
        r%

z0TestHistogramOptimBinNums.test_incorrect_methodsc
        Check that methods handle no variance in data
        Primarily for Scott and FD as the SD and IQR are both 0 in this case
        r
{0} estimator, No Variance testr
novar_datasetZ
novar_resultdictr
test_novariance
z)TestHistogramOptimBinNums.test_novariancec
        Check when IQR is 0, but variance exists, we return the sturges value
        and not the fd value.
        i
lim_var_dataZ
edges_autoZ
edges_fdZ
edges_sturgesr
test_limited_variance
z/TestHistogramOptimBinNums.test_limited_variancec
        Check the FD, Scott and Doane with outliers.
        The FD estimates a smaller binwidth since it's less affected by
        outliers. Since the range is so (artificially) large, this means more
        bins, most of which will be empty, but the data of interest usually is
        unaffected. The Scott estimator is more affected and returns fewer bins,
        despite most of the variance being in one area of the data. The Doane
        estimator lies somewhere between the other two.
        rs
hstackr
xcenterZ
outlier_datasetZ
outlier_resultdictr
test_outlier
z&TestHistogramOptimBinNums.test_outlierc
zQVerify that Scott's rule and Stone's rule converges for normally distributed datac
locZ
scale
sizer

normalr:

rngrb

d	d	d
        Straightforward testing with a mixture of linspace data (for
        consistency). Adding in a 3rd mixture that will then be
        completely ignored. All test values have been precomputed and
        the shouldn't change.
        rS
